executiveSummary.oneLiner: Hermes is a real-time web chat application supporting private messaging, group chats, and image sharing using WebSocket and REST APIs.
executiveSummary.problemStatement: Enable low-latency, real-time communication with authentication, group messaging, and media sharing using a self-hosted stack.
executiveSummary.solutionOverview: A Spring Boot backend provides REST APIs for authentication and data access, and WebSocket/STOMP for real-time messaging, paired with a React/Vite SPA frontend.
keyCapabilities: Email-based authentication with verification and password reset
knownLimitations: Message history limited to last 24 hours
Local file storage is not persistent across container rebuilds

architecture.overview: Single-page application communicating with a Spring Boot backend via REST and WebSocket/STOMP, backed by a MySQL database and an in-memory message broker.

diagram.content:
graph LR

A[Browser SPA]
B[Spring Boot API]
D[WebSocket Broker]
C[(MySQL)]

subgraph Client_Layer[Client Layer]
  A
end

  C
end

A -->|REST · HTTP/JSON| B
A -->|WebSocket · STOMP| D
D -->|Broadcast| A
B -->|JPA · Hibernate| C

components.name: Database
responsibilities: Persist users, roles, messages, groups, and file metadata

architecture.architectureRationale: WebSocket enables low-latency chat, while REST handles authentication and data retrieval. Spring Boot provides rapid development with mature security and persistence tooling.

backend.name: Spring Boot
backend.purpose: REST APIs, WebSocket handling, and application configuration
backend.tradeOffs: Higher memory footprint than minimalist frameworks

backend.name: Spring Security + JWT
backend.tradeOffs: No token revocation or refresh strategy

frontend.name: React
frontend.tradeOffs: Requires explicit state and WebSocket management

executiveSummary.oneLiner: Pokeverse is a real-time multiplayer game platform providing shared infrastructure for multiple interactive games, with a Pokémon Quiz implemented as the first game module.
executiveSummary.problemStatement: Building low-latency multiplayer games repeatedly requires solving the same problems—authentication, rooms, real-time events, chat, and state management—over and over.
executiveSummary.solutionOverview: Pokeverse provides a reusable backend platform built with Spring Boot that centralizes authentication, room management, real-time messaging, and persistence. Individual games plug into this platform as modules. A Pokémon Quiz game demonstrates single-player and multiplayer gameplay using the shared infrastructure.
primaryUsers: Hosts creating and managing multiplayer rooms
keyCapabilities: Centralized OAuth2 authentication with JWT access and refresh tokens
Reusable room and session management for multiplayer games
Shared chat system for multiplayer rooms
Pluggable game modules built on common platform services
knownLimitations: Role-Based Access Control (RBAC) is not implemented; admin access is currently handled via request parameters
Only one game module (Pokémon Quiz) is currently implemented

architecture.overview: A Next.js frontend communicates with a Spring Boot game platform via REST and WebSocket/STOMP. The backend exposes shared services for authentication, rooms, messaging, and persistence, while individual games reuse these services. PostgreSQL stores durable data and Redis supports fast state access.

graph LR

A[Next.js Client]
B[Spring Boot Game Platform]
D[WebSocket Broker]
C[(PostgreSQL)]
E[(Redis)]

subgraph Client_Layer[Client Layer]
  A
end

subgraph Application_Layer[Application Layer]
  B
  D

subgraph Persistence_Layer[Persistence Layer]

  E
end

A -->|REST · HTTP/JSON| B
A -->|WebSocket · STOMP| D
D -->|Broadcast| A
B -->|JPA · Hibernate| C
B -->|RedisTemplate| E

components.name: Database
responsibilities: Persist users, rooms, sessions, game data, and chat messages

components.name: Cache / State Store
responsibilities: Low-latency state access for active games and sessions

components.name: WebSocket Broker
responsibilities: Topic- and queue-based real-time message distribution

steps: Platform issues JWT access and refresh tokens
Client authenticates all protected REST and WebSocket requests
Platform persists results and exposes analysis
Host creates a room and players join
Game events are sent to /app over WebSocket
Platform validates and broadcasts results to subscribers
Client sends chat messages to the room channel
Messages are broadcast to room participants

architecture.architectureRationale: The platform separates stable concerns (auth, rooms, messaging, persistence) from game-specific logic. REST handles lifecycle and management flows, while WebSocket/STOMP provides low-latency real-time interaction required by multiplayer games.

shortTerm: Harden security rules for platform and WebSocket endpoints
Replace admin access via request parameters with proper authorization
midTerm: Abstract game-module interfaces for easier expansion
longTerm: Support horizontal scaling and multiple concurrent game types

executiveSummary.oneLiner: A modular, stack-based scientific calculator that parses expressions, converts to postfix, and evaluates with 50+ math functions and implicit multiplication.
executiveSummary.problemStatement: Build a learning-first calculator that demonstrates tokenization, operator precedence, and stack-based evaluation while supporting rich scientific functions.
executiveSummary.solutionOverview: A Python package exposes a `StackQueueCalculator` that tokenizes input, injects implicit multiplication, converts to postfix via the shunting-yard algorithm, and evaluates results with a function registry covering arithmetic, trig, hyperbolic, logs, combinatorics, and constants.
primaryUsers: Developers who want a programmable scientific calculator
keyCapabilities: Domain validation and safe error handling
knownLimitations: No CLI or GUI interface; usage is via Python API
No persistence layer for history or memory across runs
No complex-number support for invalid domains (e.g., sqrt(-1))

architecture.overview: A single-process Python library where expression processing flows through tokenizer, implicit multiplication, postfix conversion, then evaluation; math function groups are modularized by domain (trig, hyperbolic, math).

diagram.content:
graph TB

A[User Expression]
B[Tokenizer]
C[Implicit Multiplication]
D[Postfix Converter]
E[Postfix Evaluator]
F[Result]

A --> B --> C --> D --> E --> F

components.name: StackQueueCalculator
responsibilities: Orchestrate tokenization, postfix conversion, and evaluation

components.name: Postfix Evaluator
responsibilities: Evaluate postfix expression via stack operations

components.name: Function Modules
responsibilities: Provide math, trig, and hyperbolic helpers

steps: User passes an expression string to `evaluate()`
State is returned or cleared on demand

architecture.architectureRationale: Tokenizer + shunting-yard + postfix evaluation keeps parsing logic clear, extensible, and aligned with classic compiler techniques.

backend.name: Python
backend.purpose: Core runtime and standard math utilities
backend.tradeOffs: Single-process execution and no built-in UI

backend.name: Colorama
backend.purpose: Optional console output styling in tests

midTerm: Add persistent history and session export
Provide a simple TUI or GUI
longTerm: Introduce plugin system for custom functions
